clinicalStem: A 65-year-old woman presents to the emergency department at 11 PM with a 4-hour history of severe right-sided headache, nausea, and vomiting. She reports sudden blurring of vision in her right eye with haloes around lights. On examination, the right eye is red with a fixed, mid-dilated pupil; cornea appears hazy. Intraocular pressure measures 58 mmHg in the right eye and 14 mmHg in the left. She has no previous eye history. Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?
options: Arrange urgent ophthalmology referral for trabeculectomy tomorrow morning
Administer intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg and topical timolol 0.5% plus pilocarpine 4% immediately
Apply a patch to the right eye and prescribe oral analgesics pending outpatient review
Administer topical steroid drops and oral ibuprofen for presumed anterior uveitis
Perform urgent B-scan ultrasound to exclude posterior segment pathology before any treatment
explanation: This is acute angle-closure glaucoma (AACG), a true ophthalmic emergency. The combination of sudden painful vision loss, fixed mid-dilated pupil, hazy cornea, and IOP of 58 mmHg is diagnostic. Immediate IOP reduction is critical to prevent irreversible optic nerve damage. IV acetazolamide rapidly reduces aqueous production; topical beta-blocker (timolol) reduces production further; and pilocarpine 4% causes miosis, pulling the peripheral iris away from the trabecular meshwork to open the angle. These are given simultaneously while arranging urgent laser peripheral iridotomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Deferring surgical intervention until the next morning allows hours of optic nerve ischaemia at IOP 58 mmHg — irreversible vision loss is highly likely within hours of untreated AACG.
This is correct: simultaneous IV acetazolamide plus topical timolol and pilocarpine is the standard emergency regimen to rapidly reduce IOP in AACG.
Patching and analgesics provide no IOP-lowering effect; watchful waiting at IOP 58 mmHg will cause permanent optic nerve damage.
These features do not suggest anterior uveitis — the mid-dilated fixed pupil and acutely elevated IOP distinguish AACG; steroids would worsen the underlying pathology.
B-scan is not required before initiating urgent IOP-lowering treatment; delaying medical therapy to perform imaging in AACG wastes critical time.
clinicalPearl: Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with sudden painful vision loss, fixed mid-dilated pupil, hazy cornea, and markedly elevated IOP — it is a true emergency requiring immediate IV acetazolamide plus topical timolol and pilocarpine.
keyClue: Fixed mid-dilated pupil, hazy cornea, IOP 58 mmHg, and coloured haloes around lights are the classic quartet of acute angle-closure glaucoma.
commonExamTrap: The severe headache and vomiting can mislead toward a neurological diagnosis (e.g. subarachnoid haemorrhage); always examine the eye carefully — a red eye with fixed mid-dilated pupil points to AACG.
missedConcept: Students who fail this question have not recognised that IOP 58 mmHg requires immediate pharmacological reduction — waiting for a surgical slot causes permanent blindness.
redFlagReason: Acute angle-closure glaucoma with IOP 58 mmHg is an ophthalmic emergency — untreated, irreversible optic nerve infarction occurs within hours; immediate IOP-lowering medication must not be delayed.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man with a history of hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and hypercholesterolaemia presents with sudden, painless, complete loss of vision in his left eye that started 45 minutes ago. Visual acuity is light perception only in the left eye and 6/6 in the right. Fundoscopy of the left eye reveals a pale, whitened retina with a cherry-red spot at the fovea and attenuated arterioles. Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?
options: Urgent MRI brain to exclude occipital cortex stroke before any ocular intervention
Reassure the patient that spontaneous recovery is likely within 24 hours and arrange outpatient review
Initiate ocular massage, have the patient rebreathe into a paper bag, administer IV acetazolamide, and arrange same-day vascular and stroke team review
Start topical anti-VEGF therapy and arrange elective vitreoretinal referral within 2 weeks
Administer IV methylprednisolone for presumed giant cell arteritis and check ESR
explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) is the ocular equivalent of a stroke; the retina tolerates ischaemia for approximately 90–100 minutes before irreversible damage. Within the acute window, interventions aim to dislodge the embolus: ocular massage transiently lowers IOP and may mechanically shift the clot; CO2 rebreathing causes vasodilation; IV acetazolamide lowers IOP further to improve perfusion. Critically, CRAO is a harbinger of stroke and cardiac embolism, so same-day vascular/stroke assessment is mandatory as stroke risk is highest within 72 hours.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI brain is important to exclude concurrent stroke but should not delay immediate ocular rescue attempts within the acute ischaemic window; both can proceed in parallel.
Spontaneous recovery is extremely rare and the window for intervention is narrow (90–100 minutes); reassurance and delayed review will result in permanent blindness.
This is correct: acute IOP-lowering manoeuvres plus CO2 rebreathing plus urgent stroke workup represent the standard emergency approach within the acute window.
Anti-VEGF agents are used for wet AMD and retinal vascular complications, not for acute arterial occlusion; elective referral wastes a narrow rescue window.

Giant cell arteritis can cause CRAO, but in a 72-year-old man with AF and hypercholesterolaemia the embolic cause is far more likely; steroids alone do not address the acute occlusion and delay definitive management.
clinicalPearl: CRAO is an ocular stroke — the cherry-red spot indicates foveal choroidal perfusion against a pale ischaemic retina; act within 90 minutes and always arrange urgent stroke workup as systemic embolic risk is highest in the first 48–72 hours.
keyClue: Sudden painless monocular blindness with cherry-red spot and pale retina in an AF patient = central retinal artery occlusion; treat as an acute vascular emergency.
commonExamTrap: Students may choose MRI brain first — while important, delaying ocular rescue manoeuvres beyond the 90-minute ischaemic window eliminates any chance of visual recovery.
missedConcept: CRAO has a narrow rescue window analogous to thrombolysis in stroke; students must know the acute management steps AND the need for concurrent systemic cardiovascular workup.
redFlagReason: CRAO is an ocular emergency with a ~90-minute rescue window; it is also a harbinger of cerebral stroke with the highest stroke risk in the first 72 hours — immediate management is essential.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 2-day history of worsening right periorbital swelling, fever (39.2°C), and increasingly restricted eye movements. His parents report he had sinusitis last week. On examination, the right eye is proptotic with severe limitation of all ocular movements; he is in pain on attempted eye movement. Visual acuity in the right eye is 6/36 and declining. CT orbit shows a subperiosteal abscess along the medial orbital wall. Which is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Commence oral co-amoxiclav and arrange outpatient ophthalmology follow-up in 48 hours
Admit immediately, commence intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics, and arrange urgent surgical drainage in coordination with ENT
Apply warm compresses and topical antibiotic ointment; the swelling is likely preseptal
Perform urgent lumbar puncture to exclude cavernous sinus thrombosis before starting antibiotics
Start intravenous dexamethasone alone to reduce orbital inflammation and monitor response
explanation: This child has postseptal (orbital) cellulitis complicated by a subperiosteal abscess — confirmed by CT. Key features distinguishing this from preseptal cellulitis are proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, pain on eye movement, and reduced visual acuity. The falling VA indicates optic nerve compromise. Management requires immediate IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (covering Streptococcus, Staph aureus, and anaerobes — e.g. IV co-amoxiclav plus metronidazole) and urgent surgical drainage of the subperiosteal abscess. ENT involvement is essential as the primary source is sinusitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-up are only appropriate for uncomplicated preseptal cellulitis; this patient has a subperiosteal abscess with declining vision — oral antibiotics are inadequate and delayed intervention risks blindness or intracranial spread.
This is correct: IV antibiotics plus urgent surgical drainage is mandatory for orbital cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess causing optic nerve compromise.
Preseptal cellulitis does not cause proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, or reduced vision; applying warm compresses here is dangerous under-treatment.
Lumbar puncture should not delay starting antibiotics — treatment must not be withheld pending LP, especially with evidence of optic nerve compromise on CT.
Steroids alone without antibiotics in bacterial orbital cellulitis can mask signs of deterioration and allow intracranial spread; they have no role as monotherapy here.
clinicalPearl: Postseptal (orbital) cellulitis is distinguished from preseptal by the 'POPS' triad: Proptosis, Ophthalmoplegia, Pain on eye movement, Subsequent visual loss — a subperiosteal abscess requires IV antibiotics plus urgent surgical drainage.
keyClue: Proptosis with ophthalmoplegia, declining visual acuity, and CT-confirmed subperiosteal abscess = postseptal orbital cellulitis requiring IV antibiotics and surgical drainage.
commonExamTrap: Preseptal cellulitis can be managed with oral antibiotics; postseptal/orbital cellulitis cannot — the key differentiator is restricted eye movement and proptosis.
missedConcept: Students must distinguish preseptal from postseptal cellulitis and recognise that a subperiosteal abscess with declining vision requires urgent surgery, not conservative management.
redFlagReason: Orbital cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess and declining visual acuity is an emergency — delayed treatment risks optic nerve infarction, cavernous sinus thrombosis, and intracranial spread.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old myopic woman presents to the eye casualty clinic reporting a 6-hour history of a sudden 'curtain' coming across her right visual field from below. She also describes new floaters and photopsia in the same eye. Her visual acuity is 6/6 in the right eye on central fixation. Fundoscopy reveals a superior retinal tear with surrounding subretinal fluid extending to the superior temporal quadrant, but the macula appears intact. Which is the single most appropriate management?
options: Reassure and discharge with written advice to return if central vision is affected
Prescribe topical prednisolone drops and review in 1 week
Arrange urgent same-day vitreoretinal surgery as a macula-off emergency
Arrange urgent surgery within 24 hours to repair the macula-on retinal detachment before the macula detaches
Refer for elective laser photocoagulation within 2 weeks
explanation: This patient has a macula-on rhegmatogenous retinal detachment — the retinal detachment has not yet reached the fovea, preserving central 6/6 vision. This is an urgent surgical emergency but does NOT need same-day emergency treatment in the way a macula-off detachment does. The goal is to operate within 24 hours (next available theatre slot) before the subretinal fluid progresses to involve the macula. If the macula detaches, visual prognosis worsens significantly. Scleral buckling or pars plana vitrectomy with gas tamponade are the surgical options.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassurance is dangerous — a macula-on detachment that is not operated on urgently will progress to macula-off, significantly worsening prognosis.
Topical steroids have no role in rhegmatogenous retinal detachment; this is a mechanical problem requiring surgical intervention.
Same-day emergency surgery is required for macula-off detachment (to limit foveal ischaemia); this is macula-on, so 24-hour urgency is appropriate — though still very urgent.
This is correct: macula-on retinal detachment requires surgery within 24 hours to prevent progression to macula-off and preserve central vision.
Laser photocoagulation alone is insufficient for retinal detachment with subretinal fluid — it is used for retinal tears without detachment to create a barrier.
clinicalPearl: Macula-on retinal detachment requires surgery within 24 hours to prevent progression and preserve central vision; macula-off requires same-day emergency surgery to minimise foveal damage.
keyClue: 6/6 central vision despite a 'curtain' from below = macula-on retinal detachment — urgently surgical but the 24-hour window is the target rather than immediate emergency.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse macula-on (24-hour urgency) with macula-off (same-day emergency); always check central VA — if intact, the macula has not detached yet.
missedConcept: The distinction between macula-on and macula-off retinal detachment determines surgical urgency and visual prognosis — this is a high-yield exam concept.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man with ankylosing spondylitis presents with a 2-day history of acute onset left eye pain, photophobia, and blurred vision. Examination reveals circumcorneal (ciliary) injection, miosis, and a hypopyon (layered white cells in the anterior chamber). Slit-lamp examination shows fine keratic precipitates and posterior synechiae. Intraocular pressure is 22 mmHg. Which is the single most appropriate initial management?
options: Topical ciprofloxacin four times daily for bacterial conjunctivitis
Intensive topical corticosteroids (prednisolone 1% hourly) plus topical cyclopentolate to break posterior synechiae
Oral prednisolone 60 mg daily as monotherapy for 2 weeks
Immediate vitreoretinal referral for pars plana vitrectomy
Topical timolol 0.5% twice daily to manage elevated intraocular pressure
explanation: This is acute anterior uveitis (AAU) — a well-recognised extra-articular feature of HLA-B27-associated ankylosing spondylitis. The management priorities are: (1) suppress inflammation with intensive topical corticosteroids (prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone drops hourly) and (2) dilate the pupil with a cycloplegic agent (cyclopentolate or atropine) to relieve ciliary spasm, reduce pain, and break posterior synechiae (iris adherent to lens). Untreated posterior synechiae can lead to pupil block glaucoma. The IOP at 22 mmHg here is likely inflammation-related and will respond to steroid treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This presentation has circumcorneal (not diffuse) injection, KPs, cells in the AC, and hypopyon — these features exclude simple bacterial conjunctivitis; topical antibiotics are inappropriate.
This is correct: intensive topical steroids and cycloplegics are the cornerstone of AAU management — steroids reduce inflammation; cycloplegics break synechiae and relieve pain.
Systemic steroids may be needed in severe or non-responsive cases but are not first-line monotherapy for acute anterior uveitis, which responds well to topical treatment.
Pars plana vitrectomy is a posterior segment procedure for vitreous disorders; it has no role in the acute management of anterior uveitis.
Treating only the IOP with a beta-blocker without addressing the underlying inflammation will allow ongoing anterior segment damage, synechiae formation, and vision loss.
clinicalPearl: HLA-B27-associated anterior uveitis is treated with intensive topical corticosteroids and cycloplegics — the cycloplegic is critical to break posterior synechiae and prevent pupil block glaucoma.
keyClue: Ankylosing spondylitis + circumcorneal injection + hypopyon + posterior synechiae = HLA-B27 anterior uveitis requiring topical steroids plus cycloplegics.
commonExamTrap: IOP of 22 mmHg may tempt treatment with a beta-blocker first; however, the inflammation itself is raising IOP and topical steroids are the priority — treat the disease, not just the IOP.
missedConcept: Cycloplegics are not just for comfort — they actively break posterior synechiae and prevent the devastating complication of pupil block glaucoma.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman with known Graves' disease presents with a 5-day history of worsening bilateral proptosis, diplopia, and periorbital oedema. Today she notices that colours seem washed out and she is struggling to read. Visual acuity is 6/36 right and 6/24 left. Colour desaturation is confirmed on Ishihara testing. Hertel exophthalmometry shows 26 mm bilaterally. CT orbit reveals enlargement of all extra-ocular muscle bellies with apical crowding compressing both optic nerves. Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?
options: Prescribe selenium supplementation and refer to endocrinology for thyroid optimisation over 3 months
Start lubricating eye drops and an eye pad at night for corneal exposure
Administer high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 500 mg daily for 3 days and arrange urgent orbital decompression surgery if no improvement within 48 hours
Apply orbital radiotherapy as the only definitive treatment for apical crowding
Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily and review in 4 weeks as an outpatient
explanation: This woman has dysthyroid optic neuropathy (DON) — the most sight-threatening complication of thyroid eye disease. Colour desaturation and VA decline indicate optic nerve compression by the enlarged muscle bellies at the orbital apex. DON is an emergency: IV methylprednisolone (500 mg/day for 3 consecutive days) is the first-line treatment to rapidly reduce inflammation and oedema. If VA does not improve within 48 hours, urgent surgical orbital decompression (enlarging the bony orbit to relieve apical compression) is required to save sight.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Selenium is used for mild, early-stage thyroid eye disease (CAS ≥3, mild activity) — it has no role in acute dysthyroid optic neuropathy where vision is deteriorating.
Lubricants and eye pads address corneal exposure complications of proptosis but do nothing to relieve optic nerve compression; vision will continue to deteriorate.
This is correct: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy is the emergency treatment of choice for DON, with surgical decompression as rescue therapy if there is no response.
Radiotherapy is an adjunct for reducing muscle inflammation and motility restriction; it acts slowly (weeks) and cannot provide the urgent IOP relief needed in acute optic nerve compression.
Oral steroids have a slower onset and lower peak concentration than IV pulse therapy; in acute DON with failing vision, oral prednisolone is insufficient and dangerous delays worsen prognosis.
clinicalPearl: Dysthyroid optic neuropathy is the most serious complication of thyroid eye disease — colour desaturation is the earliest sign; IV methylprednisolone pulse is the emergency treatment, followed by orbital decompression if vision does not recover within 48 hours.
keyClue: Colour desaturation plus worsening VA with CT apical crowding in Graves' disease = dysthyroid optic neuropathy — an ophthalmic emergency.
commonExamTrap: Students underestimate thyroid eye disease and choose slow outpatient treatments; colour desaturation and VA decline are warning signs of optic nerve compression requiring emergency intervention.
missedConcept: The hallmark of DON is colour desaturation preceding VA loss; IV rather than oral steroids are required because of the speed of onset needed.
redFlagReason: Dysthyroid optic neuropathy with declining VA and colour desaturation is an emergency — untreated optic nerve compression progresses to permanent blindness within days.

clinicalStem: A 60-year-old woman is referred by her optician after routine examination revealed cup-to-disc ratios of 0.8 bilaterally and inferior notching of the left optic disc rim. Her IOP readings are consistently 16 mmHg in both eyes (within normal limits). Visual field testing shows an arcuate scotoma in the left eye. She is otherwise healthy with no family history of glaucoma. OCT confirms retinal nerve fibre layer thinning in the left inferior quadrant. Which is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassure and discharge — intraocular pressure is normal so she cannot have glaucoma
Refer for immediate laser peripheral iridotomy
Commence IOP-lowering therapy (topical prostaglandin analogue) with a target of 20–30% IOP reduction and arrange regular monitoring
Start oral acetazolamide long-term to achieve maximal IOP reduction
Arrange vitreoretinal referral for the arcuate scotoma as this represents retinal disease, not glaucoma
explanation: This patient has normal-tension glaucoma (NTG) — glaucomatous optic nerve damage (cupping, RNFL thinning, scotoma) despite IOP within the statistically normal range. The Collaborative Normal-Tension Glaucoma Study demonstrated that reducing IOP by 30% (even from normal levels) slows progression significantly. A topical prostaglandin analogue (e.g. latanoprost) is first-line, achieving around 25–35% IOP reduction. Regular monitoring of fields and OCT is essential to detect progression.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Normal IOP does not exclude glaucoma — NTG accounts for approximately 30–40% of open-angle glaucoma in Western populations; discharging this patient risks unopposed progressive vision loss.
Laser peripheral iridotomy is the treatment for angle-closure glaucoma; this patient has open-angle glaucoma (NTG); LPI is inappropriate.
This is correct: IOP-lowering therapy with a prostaglandin analogue targeting a 30% reduction is the evidence-based management for normal-tension glaucoma.
Oral acetazolamide carries significant systemic side-effects (metabolic acidosis, renal calculi, paraesthesiae) and is reserved for acute IOP crises, not long-term NTG management.
The arcuate scotoma correlates perfectly with inferior RNFL thinning and optic disc notching — this is glaucomatous field loss, not a retinal vascular or structural lesion.
clinicalPearl: Normal-tension glaucoma is diagnosed when optic nerve damage and visual field loss occur with IOP in the normal range; IOP-lowering therapy still slows progression — the CNTGS proved a 30% IOP reduction is protective.
keyClue: Cup-to-disc ratio 0.8, inferior disc notching, arcuate scotoma, and RNFL thinning despite IOP of 16 mmHg = normal-tension glaucoma.
commonExamTrap: Normal IOP does not exclude glaucoma — up to 40% of open-angle glaucoma occurs at normal pressures; never dismiss optic nerve damage based on IOP alone.
missedConcept: Students may not know that treating NTG with IOP-lowering drops is evidence-based even when IOP is statistically normal — the target is a 30% reduction from baseline.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old man presents complaining of metamorphopsia (distorted vision) and rapid central visual loss in his right eye over 4 weeks. His left eye has a visual acuity of 6/9 and his right eye 6/36. Fundoscopy of the right eye reveals subretinal haemorrhage and grey-green subretinal elevation at the fovea. OCT confirms subretinal fluid with choroidal neovascularisation. Fluorescein angiography shows classic occult leakage. Which is the single most effective treatment to preserve and potentially improve his central vision?
options: Thermal laser photocoagulation of the subfoveal membrane to destroy the neovascular complex
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection (e.g. ranibizumab or bevacizumab) with a loading phase of 3 monthly injections
Oral zinc and antioxidant vitamins (AREDS formula) as definitive treatment
Immediate pars plana vitrectomy to drain the subretinal haemorrhage
Photodynamic therapy with verteporfin as first-line treatment for all types of CNV
explanation: This patient has neovascular (wet) age-related macular degeneration (nAMD) — the leading cause of central vision loss in the elderly. The standard of care is intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy: ranibizumab (Lucentis), bevacizumab (Avastin), or aflibercept (Eylea) are injected intravitreally. A loading dose of 3 monthly injections is given, followed by a treat-and-extend or PRN regimen. Anti-VEGF not only halts progression but can improve VA by 2–3 lines in up to 40% of treated patients, making it the only treatment that can improve as well as preserve vision.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thermal laser photocoagulation ablates the retina overlying the CNV membrane — it destroys the macula when the lesion is subfoveal and is only used for extrafoveal lesions; it cannot improve vision.
This is correct: intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy is the gold-standard treatment for wet AMD — it inhibits choroidal neovascularisation and can both preserve and improve central vision.
AREDS formula supplements reduce risk of progression from intermediate to advanced dry AMD; they have no therapeutic role in established wet AMD with active CNV.
Vitrectomy is not indicated as primary treatment for wet AMD; surgical drainage of subretinal haemorrhage may rarely be required in massive haemorrhage but is not first-line.
Photodynamic therapy with verteporfin was the standard before anti-VEGF; it is now reserved for specific subtypes (e.g. polypoidal CNV not responding to anti-VEGF) and does not improve vision.
clinicalPearl: Wet AMD with active CNV is treated with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections — the only treatment that can improve as well as preserve central vision; early treatment preserves the most photoreceptors.
keyClue: Metamorphopsia + subretinal haemorrhage + OCT-confirmed CNV in an elderly patient = wet AMD requiring urgent anti-VEGF therapy.
commonExamTrap: AREDS supplements are for dry AMD prevention; students must not confuse them with active treatment for wet AMD with established CNV.
missedConcept: Anti-VEGF is the only treatment for wet AMD that can both preserve and improve vision; earlier treatment means better outcomes — do not delay.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl is referred by her GP after her parents noticed a left convergent squint. Her visual acuities are 6/6 right and 6/24 left. Cycloplegic refraction reveals +3.5 D hypermetropia bilaterally. Cover testing confirms a constant left esotropia of 25 prism dioptres. There is no history of double vision and neurological examination is normal. Which is the single most appropriate initial management?
options: Arrange strabismus surgery immediately to correct the cosmetic deviation
Reassure the parents — children outgrow squints and no treatment is needed before age 7
Prescribe the full hypermetropic correction in spectacles and commence occlusion therapy of the right (dominant) eye for 2–4 hours daily to treat amblyopia
Commence botulinum toxin injection into the medial rectus as first-line treatment
Refer for urgent MRI brain to exclude a sixth nerve palsy as the cause of the esotropia
explanation: This child has accommodative esotropia secondary to uncorrected hypermetropia — the effort to accommodate for the refractive error drives excessive convergence. The cornerstone of management is: (1) full cycloplegic refraction with spectacle correction (which alone may correct the esotropia fully in accommodative cases) and (2) occlusion therapy (patching the dominant/better-seeing eye) to treat the amblyopia (lazy eye). Amblyopia treatment is time-critical — the visual cortex is plastic only until approximately 7–8 years of age; untreated amblyopia leads to permanent reduced vision.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Surgery is considered after glasses and amblyopia treatment have been optimised — operating on a child with amblyopia before treating the lazy eye may correct the angle but will not improve vision; cosmesis without vision is suboptimal.
Children do NOT outgrow squints; untreated constant esotropia leads to irreversible amblyopia. The critical period for visual cortex plasticity closes around age 7–8 — early treatment is essential.
This is correct: full hypermetropic correction addresses the accommodative component and occlusion therapy of the dominant eye forces the amblyopic eye to develop, treating the reduced vision.
Botulinum toxin injection is an alternative to strabismus surgery; it is not used as first-line treatment before optical correction and amblyopia treatment have been tried.

Bilateral hypermetropia-driven accommodative esotropia in a healthy child with no neurological signs does not require MRI brain; an abducens palsy would cause an exotropia (divergence failure), not esotropia.
clinicalPearl: Accommodative esotropia in children is managed with full spectacle correction of hypermetropia plus occlusion therapy for amblyopia — treatment must begin before age 7–8 when cortical plasticity closes.
keyClue: 4-year-old with constant esotropia, high bilateral hypermetropia, and 6/24 in the squinting eye = accommodative esotropia with amblyopia requiring glasses and patching.
commonExamTrap: Students may choose surgery first; the rule is treat amblyopia before operating — post-operative binocularity requires both eyes to have good vision.
missedConcept: The critical period for amblyopia treatment closes at approximately 7–8 years; delay means permanent visual loss in the amblyopic eye regardless of later surgical correction.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a sudden onset complete right ptosis, and when the eyelid is lifted, the right eye is found to be looking 'down and out' with a fully dilated, unreactive right pupil. There is no pain. Blood pressure is 168/104 mmHg and random blood glucose is 14.2 mmol/L. He reports no headache or neck stiffness. Which is the single most important next investigation?
options: Fasting lipid profile and HbA1c to confirm diabetic microvascular third nerve palsy
Urgent CT angiography or MRI/MRA of the brain to exclude a posterior communicating artery aneurysm
Reassure and discharge with outpatient neurology referral in 2 weeks as this is typical diabetic mononeuropathy
Arrange urgent carotid Doppler as carotid artery disease is the most common cause
Perform a Tensilon (edrophonium) test to exclude myasthenia gravis
explanation: A complete third nerve palsy with pupil involvement (dilated, unreactive) is a neurosurgical emergency until proven otherwise. The dilated pupil signals compression of the pupillomotor fibres, which travel on the outside of cranial nerve III and are the first to be compressed by an expanding aneurysm — classically a posterior communicating artery (PCoA) aneurysm. Microvascular (diabetic) third nerve palsy typically SPARES the pupil. Urgent CT angiography (or MRI/MRA if CT angiography is unavailable) is mandatory to exclude an aneurysm requiring neurosurgical intervention before rupture.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Diabetic microvascular third nerve palsy typically spares the pupil because the ischaemia affects the central core of the nerve (motor fibres) while sparing the peripherally-situated pupillomotor fibres; a dilated unreactive pupil excludes this as the likely cause.
This is correct: pupil-involving complete CN III palsy mandates urgent CT angiography or MRI/MRA to exclude a life-threatening PCoA aneurysm before discharge.
Discharging a patient with a pupil-involving third nerve palsy is dangerous — if a PCoA aneurysm ruptures before diagnosis, the risk of subarachnoid haemorrhage and death is extremely high.
Carotid artery disease causes anterior circulation TIAs and Horner syndrome, not a complete third nerve palsy with pupil dilation; carotid Doppler is not the priority here.
Myasthenia gravis causes variable ptosis and ophthalmoplegia but does NOT affect the pupil — a dilated unreactive pupil excludes myasthenia; Tensilon testing is not relevant here.
clinicalPearl: Pupil-involving third nerve palsy = aneurysm until proven otherwise — urgent CT angiography or MRI/MRA is mandatory; diabetic CN III palsy spares the pupil by affecting the central ischaemic core while preserving peripherally-placed pupillomotor fibres.
keyClue: Complete CN III palsy (ptosis + 'down and out' + dilated unreactive pupil) — pupil involvement is the red flag that mandates urgent vascular imaging to exclude PCoA aneurysm.
commonExamTrap: Diabetes and hypertension suggest a vascular cause, but pupil involvement overrides this — never attribute a pupil-involving CN III palsy to diabetes without excluding aneurysm by imaging.
missedConcept: The pupil-sparing rule: diabetic CN III affects the vascular core; aneurysm compresses the outer pupillomotor fibres first — pupil involvement is always a red flag for compressive cause.
redFlagReason: Pupil-involving CN III palsy may be the herald sign of a posterior communicating artery aneurysm about to rupture; failure to perform urgent vascular imaging risks fatal subarachnoid haemorrhage.

clinicalStem: A 7-month-old boy is brought to the paediatric outpatient clinic by his mother, who reports persistent watering (epiphora) and mucoid discharge from his right eye since birth. The eye is not red and there is no associated fever. Regurgitation of mucoid material is elicited on pressure over the lacrimal sac. The cornea is clear. Which is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate lacrimal sac probing and irrigation under general anaesthesia
Prescribe prolonged topical chloramphenicol and review at 5 years
Lacrimal massage (Crigler technique) of the nasolacrimal sac combined with topical antibiotic drops during purulent episodes, with review at 12 months
CT orbit to evaluate the nasolacrimal duct anatomy before any treatment
Referral for dacryocystorhinostomy as first-line surgical management
explanation: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction (CNLDO) affects approximately 6% of newborns and is caused by failure of canalisation of the nasolacrimal duct at the valve of Hasner. The vast majority (90–95%) resolve spontaneously by 12 months of age. First-line management is lacrimal massage (Crigler technique — firm downward pressure over the nasolacrimal sac to increase hydrostatic pressure and rupture the membrane) combined with antibiotic drops for infectious episodes. Probing is reserved for those not resolved by 12–13 months.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Probing under GA is effective but invasive; spontaneous resolution rate is approximately 90% by 12 months, so immediate surgery at 7 months is premature and not evidence-based first-line management.
Prolonged topical antibiotics alone do not address the obstruction and reviewing at age 5 is too late — the window for probing under local anaesthesia is 12–24 months.
This is correct: Crigler massage plus antibiotic drops during infectious episodes is the evidence-based conservative management, with resolution expected by 12 months in the majority.
CT orbit is not required for straightforward CNLDO in an infant — it involves ionising radiation and is reserved for complex or atypical cases with suspected structural anomalies.
Dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) is reserved for failed probing or complex obstruction in older children/adults; it is not first-line for congenital NLD obstruction in an infant.
clinicalPearl: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction resolves spontaneously in 90% of cases by 12 months — first-line treatment is Crigler lacrimal massage; probing is reserved for those not resolved by 12–13 months.
keyClue: Unilateral watering with mucoid discharge from birth, regurgitation on lacrimal sac pressure, and a clear cornea = congenital NLD obstruction requiring conservative management first.
commonExamTrap: Students may choose immediate surgical probing — it is effective but premature; spontaneous resolution is the rule at this age and probing is reserved for persistence beyond 12 months.
missedConcept: CNLDO has a 90% spontaneous resolution rate by age 12 months; unnecessary early surgery exposes infants to GA risk without evidence of benefit over watchful waiting with massage.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with no significant medical history presents with 3 days of binocular horizontal diplopia that is worse on right gaze. He reports a dull headache that has been present for 6 days. Examination reveals inability to abduct the right eye past the midline; all other ocular movements are intact. There is no ptosis and the pupil is equal and reactive bilaterally. Blood pressure is 130/82 mmHg and blood glucose is 5.1 mmol/L. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. Which is the single most important management priority?
options: Prescribe a Fresnel prism and refer to neuro-ophthalmology outpatient clinic in 4–6 weeks
Reassure — sixth nerve palsies in adults are almost always microvascular and self-limiting within 3 months
Arrange urgent MRI brain with gadolinium to exclude raised intracranial pressure, cavernous sinus pathology, or an intracranial mass
Measure erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein to exclude giant cell arteritis
Arrange fasting glucose and HbA1c — this is almost certainly a diabetic mononeuropathy
explanation: Sixth nerve (abducens) palsy in an otherwise healthy, normoglycaemic, normotensive adult without identifiable risk factors should be treated as a sign of raised intracranial pressure or intracranial mass lesion until proven otherwise — the abducens nerve has the longest intracranial course and is a 'false localising sign' in raised ICP. The 6-day headache preceding the diplopia is a red flag. Urgent MRI brain with gadolinium is required to exclude raised ICP (hydrocephalus, space-occupying lesion, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis), cavernous sinus pathology, or brainstem lesions.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Prismatic correction addresses the symptom of diplopia but does not investigate the cause; in a non-vasculopathic patient with headache, deferring investigation risks missing a serious intracranial cause.

While microvascular CN VI palsy is common in elderly diabetics and hypertensives, this 45-year-old has no vascular risk factors and has a preceding headache — this cannot be assumed to be microvascular without excluding structural pathology.
This is correct: urgent MRI brain is mandatory in a non-vasculopathic patient with a sixth nerve palsy and preceding headache to exclude raised ICP, SOL, or cavernous sinus disease.
GCA causes anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy and CRAO in elderly patients; it is extremely rare in a 45-year-old and does not typically present as an isolated sixth nerve palsy.
Normal blood glucose and no diabetic history make diabetic mononeuropathy very unlikely; assuming this without imaging in a patient with headache is dangerous practice.
clinicalPearl: Sixth nerve palsy is a false localising sign of raised intracranial pressure — in any patient without clear vasculopathic risk factors, or when accompanied by headache, urgent MRI brain is mandatory to exclude an intracranial cause.
keyClue: Isolated sixth nerve palsy in a normoglycaemic, normotensive patient with a preceding headache — the headache is the red flag that demands urgent neuroimaging.
commonExamTrap: Students assume CN VI palsy = microvascular; the absence of vascular risk factors and the presence of headache must trigger investigation for raised ICP or structural lesion.
missedConcept: Sixth nerve palsy is a classic false localising sign because the nerve's long intracranial course makes it susceptible to any cause of raised ICP — always image the young or vascular-risk-free patient.
redFlagReason: Sixth nerve palsy with preceding headache in a non-vasculopathic patient may indicate raised intracranial pressure, intracranial mass, or cerebral venous sinus thrombosis — urgent MRI is required to exclude life-threatening pathology.
